name: getIdTokenClaims hook for custom ID token claims
outcome: Consumers can add custom claims (roles, tenant, acr) to issued ID tokens without being able to corrupt the protocol claims or the JOSE header.
scope: Wire the optional getIdTokenClaims hook into the ID token builder with an explicit strip-then-merge so protocol claims always win — by named constant, not by spread-ordering trust. Hook output reaches the JWT payload only, never the JOSE header.
acceptance_criteria: Custom claims returned by getIdTokenClaims appear in the issued id_token's payload
PROTOCOL_CLAIM_NAMES is an exported named constant — not an inline literal — and lists exactly: iss, sub, aud, exp, iat, at_hash, nonce, auth_time

The hook merge strips PROTOCOL_CLAIM_NAMES from the hook's return value before merging. A hook that returns { nonce, at_hash, roles: ['admin'] } produces an id_token whose nonce and at_hash are the protocol values, roles is the consumer value
Hook output reaches the JWT payload only — never the JOSE header. A hook returning { alg: 'HS256', typ: 'fake' } cannot influence what jsonwebtoken signs with
When the hook is absent the id_token contains exactly the protocol claim set
Hook errors propagate as OAuthException.invalidGrant rather than being swallowed (consumer mistakes are visible)
steps: ID token builder — define and export PROTOCOL_CLAIM_NAMES as a named const array (the eight names above)
ID token builder — call the optional getIdTokenClaims hook with { userId, scopes, client, nonce }

ID token builder — strip PROTOCOL_CLAIM_NAMES from the hook output, then merge the stripped object into the payload (not spread-ordering). The merge structure must make the strip explicit; do not rely on spread order to be 'safe enough'
ID token builder — pass the merged payload to jwt.sign with the JOSE header fixed at { typ: 'JWT' }; hook output never reaches SignOptions

test/unit — the strip-then-merge guard with each PROTOCOL_CLAIM_NAMES member as the attacker key (eight tests); a hook returning header-shaped keys cannot affect the header; absent hook yields exactly the protocol set; a throwing hook surfaces as invalid_grant
notes: Re-pointed 1 → 2 because the security-sensitive merge guard with eight attacker-key tests and the header-isolation test are real work, not a one-step wire-through. The hook keeps the default ID token lean while removing the hard wall; it also satisfies the deferred claims-in-ID-token need without a separate flag.
